So we have a set of components or processes, And we need to identify somebody who can coordinate some activity for us, whether it's to decide who gets a lock for a particular resource, whether it is to decide on the order potentially of received messages in a multicast, different needs we will have for leaders to make things correct. And associated with that, we talked about the general issue of mutual exclusion and different algorithms for doing that, one of which requires a leader. And then we'll move into qualitative consistency, which isn't based on numeric calculations, but based on essentially orders of things, when things happen. The case that we're most concerned about, we'll call it case one, is we have replicated data. And by that we mean the data is replicated and it is being managed by processing in different locations. Okay. Think of different servers holding copies of some kind of data set. And it gets interesting and tricky when that data can be updated. Data were never changing, like delivering a Netflix video from 14,000 locations, we wouldn't have a consistency problem. We're just reading the data, or we're just viewing the data. But imagine, you know, we're talking about timelines and things where people can change. things or email receiving and sending, which can change what the data is, adds more complications or bank accounts. We've used that example a few times. And so first question is, okay, why is data replicated? Why do we have data in different locations? And the principal reason, there's really two reasons, probably the first one's the principal one, is it's replicated for availability. If a replica goes down or is unreachable, then we still want the data to be accessible to a client. So that's a sort of a fault tolerance argument. The other argument that you will see is data is replicated for performance. If you go to CNN website, there's not a single server running that website. there's probably hundreds of thousands of machines running it because of the demand.

So naturally the data is going to be replicated across those machines because we want to direct all the millions of users coming in to different machines to get the data, get to web pages. So that's sort of scaling in numbers. We have a large load, a lot of interest in the data, and we want to achieve as much kind of concurrency or throughput as we can in terms of all the users. We wouldn't want all the clients stacking up one behind each other trying to view a popular web page, for example. And then replication also gives us an opportunity to think about where we're scaling and we'll talk about how much and where more on Wednesday, I think. But then we can also scale geographically. And a good example is the replication that content distribution networks do, like, for example, Netflix. They're going to put replicas at specific locations meant to be nearer to users. So if I watch a Netflix video in Hong Kong, presumably some CDN, some server close to where I am in Hong Kong, will serve me the video. I'll probably have to use a VPN to get around the restrictions. But anyway, I do that.
